did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a prototype skin cancer detector (CBMelanom) for Android phones using Case-Based Reasoning (CBR), a subfield of artificial intelligence. The system utilized the DePicT concept to combine image classification and textual information from patient health records. It employed the myCBR tool to implement CBR algorithms for case retrieval, similarity measurement, and recommendation of solutions for melanoma detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents the development and implementation of a prototype system named CBMelanom for Android phones using Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) and the DePicT concept. It describes the system's architecture, methodology (including case formation, retrieval, and similarity measurement), and functional components. The abstract states the purpose is to 'develop the case-based system' and the conclusion summarizes the results of this research project, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review of prior literature.

title: A Prototype of Case-based Skin Cancer Detector for Android Phones based on DePicT Concept: CBMelanom
journal: 2016 International Conference on Computational Science and Computational Intelligence
brief_summary: This study presents CBMelanom, a prototype Android application designed for early skin cancer detection using Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) and the DePicT concept. The system integrates image classification of skin lesions with textual patient health records through a conversational question-dialog interface. It utilizes the myCBR tool to manage case retrieval, similarity measurement, and recommendation of solutions based on stored cases. The application allows users to capture images and answer questions to create a case, which is then compared against a database of existing cases. Domain experts can validate and update the case base to ensure accuracy. The authors conclude that the system is functional for skin disease detection and can empower patients in self-care, though it requires expansion with more cases and improved similarity measures.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on developing a prototype detector (CBMelanom) specifically for early detection of melanoma. The system is described as a 'Melanoma detector' and the case base contains cases related to melanoma skin cancer. The study cites statistics regarding melanoma deaths to justify the need for early detection.

all_task_types.details: The paper describes a prototype system (CBMelanom) for 'detecting the skin cancer' and specifically 'Melanoma detector'. The system utilizes 'image classification' as part of the DePicT concept to identify skin problems. The output is a recommendation/solution based on classifying the user's case against existing cases in the case base, effectively performing a binary or multi-class diagnostic classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma or specific melanoma types) rather than predicting a continuous numeric value.

datasets_used.name: CBMelanom Case Base (Prototype)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary, manually constructed case base created for the prototype CBMelanom Android application. It consists of cases containing patient images (photos of affected areas), textual health records (answers to a question-dialog), and associated solutions/recommendations for melanoma detection. The cases were created by domain experts and loaded into the myCBR tool for testing case retrieval.
classes_used: Non-Melanoma (implied by 'skin diseases domain' and binary detection context)

use.training: The case base was populated with cases created by domain experts to test the case retrieval functionality of the myCBR tool. It served as the reference library for the Case-Based Reasoning system.
use.validation: Domain experts validated and verified the case base and knowledge models to ensure accuracy and consistency.
use.testing: The case base was used to test the case retrieval mechanism of the prototype application.
use.other: The system is described as a prototype requiring expansion with more cases; no specific external benchmark dataset was used for evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study describes the development and functional testing of a prototype system (CBMelanom) using Case-Based Reasoning, focusing on system architecture, case retrieval, and similarity measurement rather than statistical hypothesis testing.

CBMelanom Prototype Case Base.notes: The paper describes the development and functional testing of the CBMelanom prototype. It states that 'unit testing and evaluation of CBMelanom functionality shows that it can be utilized in skin diseases domain'. The system retrieves similar cases and displays their 'similarity degree' to the user. However, no quantitative performance metrics (such as accuracy, precision, recall, or specific similarity scores) are reported for the case retrieval results. The case base was populated with cases created by domain experts to test case retrieval, but specific numerical results of these tests are not provided.

architecture_used.main_model: Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) system using the DePicT concept (Detect and Predict diseases using image classification and Text Information)

architecture_details.framework: myCBR tool (open-source CBR tool for rapid-prototyping)
architecture_details.approach: Conversational Case-Based Reasoning (CCBR) with a question-dialog interface
architecture_details.data_types: Graphical data (images of affected areas) and textual data (patient health records, answers to questions)
architecture_details.similarity_measure: Local similarity measure based on symbolic attributes using a symmetric similarity table
architecture_details.case_structure: Predefined structure summarizing symptoms, reference images, and test results
architecture_details.implementation: Client-server architecture; Android app (client) communicates with a server via ObjectOutputStream/ClientInputStream; SQLite database for data persistence

training_details.method: Manual case creation and validation by domain experts
training_details.dataset_preparation: Cases created by domain experts and loaded into the myCBR tool for testing case retrieval
training_details.notes: No automated training or hyperparameter optimization reported; system relies on expert-defined knowledge models and similarity tables

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Recommendation/Solution based on retrieved similar cases
classification_or_output_head.details: Displays CBR-Casename, similarity degree, problem description, and solution of the most similar cases

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CBMelanom (prototype system using CBR and DePicT concept)
myCBR tool (used for implementing CBR algorithms)
preprocessing_techniques: Feature selection and analysis of patient data (textual and graphical) for CBR recommendation methodology
Validation and verification of collected information by domain experts
Updating or correcting patient data records by domain experts
Creation of symmetric similarity tables for symbolic attributes to define local similarity measures
conclusions: The CBMelanom prototype, utilizing Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) and the DePicT concept, was successfully developed and implemented as an Android application for early melanoma detection.
Unit testing and evaluation demonstrated that the CBMelanom system is functional and can be utilized in the skin diseases domain.
The system empowers patients in self-care by allowing them to capture images of affected areas and answer questions via a conversational interface to receive recommendations.
The system supports domain experts in validating, verifying, and updating the case base and knowledge models to ensure accuracy.
The current CBR system requires extension by collecting more cases containing problems and solutions to improve its robustness.
Future work includes implementing adaptation rules to enrich recommended solutions and enhancing similarity measurement based on new test results to improve the retrieval phase.
important_remarks: The study presents a prototype system rather than a validated clinical tool; no quantitative performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) were reported.
The case base was manually constructed by domain experts for testing purposes, with no specified size or external validation dataset.
The system relies on a small, proprietary case base created for testing, limiting generalizability and indicating a need for expansion with more cases.
Similarity measures are based on symbolic attributes using symmetric similarity tables, which may not capture complex visual features of skin lesions effectively.
The paper acknowledges that the current CBR system requires extension by collecting more cases and implementing adaptation rules to improve robustness.
No external validation was performed; the system was evaluated only through unit testing and functional evaluation of the prototype.
The system is designed for early detection and self-care empowerment but lacks evidence of clinical efficacy or comparison with existing diagnostic standards.
